topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical weights pressing down on Elias. He stared at the canvas, a vibrant swirl of indigo and rust, the culmination of three sleepless nights. Then he saw it—a faint, almost invisible line tracing the edge of the composition, a deliberate mark that wasn't part of his brushwork. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, a familiar, sour feeling rising in his chest. He traced the line with a trembling finger, his breath catching in his throat. It was a signature, crude and undeniable. He looked up, scanning the crowded room, his eyes locking onto a figure near the entrance, a woman whose smile seemed too knowing, too smug. He felt a sudden, sharp urge to crumple the painting, to destroy the evidence of his labor, but his hands remained frozen, stiff with a simmering indignation.

[story 2]

"You really think that's clever?" Marcus scoffed, leaning against the easel, his voice dripping with false admiration. He watched as Clara, his muse, meticulously adjusted the framing of his latest abstract piece. When she finally stepped back, a flash of dark ink caught his eye—a small, stylized raven tucked just beneath the lower border. A wave of profound irritation washed over him, making his jaw clench. He felt a distinct heat spreading across his neck. He didn't need to ask; the implication hung heavy in the air. He took a slow sip of his lukewarm wine, his gaze fixed on the raven, his knuckles whitening around the glass. He wanted to confront her, to demand an explanation, but a strange paralysis held him captive, a stubborn refusal to let his composure crack.

[story 3]

I spent the afternoon meticulously cleaning my brushes, the rhythmic scrubbing a poor substitute for the churning feeling inside me. The portrait, my masterpiece, hung center stage. And there, just beneath the painted iris, was a tiny, perfectly rendered depiction of a thorny vine. It wasn't mine. It belonged to someone else. A deep, ugly churning started low in my gut, a heavy pressure that made breathing shallow. I walked over, my footsteps echoing loudly on the polished floor. I didn't look at the painting; I looked only at the tattoo, my face tightening into a mask of controlled fury. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously low, stripped bare of all warmth. "Who did this?"
